# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for a Mind-Bending Movie You’ll Want to Watch Twice

**Score:** 58
**Body:** I’m looking for a movie that you need to watch twice 

The first time to simply enjoy it,

The second time to truly understand the deeper perspective of the characters.

I know that asking for such recommendations can slightly spoil the surprise, but I’m specifically searching for films similar to Fight Club or The Sixth Sense — movies that completely shock you at the end because you never saw it coming.

I want something with a powerful twist that changes your entire understanding of the story once it’s revealed.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Arrival (2016) about the aliens who come to Earth and experts try to understand them and develop ways to communicate with them.
2. The Prestige from Christopher Nolan, movie gets richer every time you watch it.
  - I've watched 6 times and every time I've noticed more and more that I had ignored that was intentionally right in your face the whole time. So, again... "Are you watching closely?"
3. Memento
4. Donnie Darko

The director's cut of The Butterfly Effect (2004)
5. Eternal sunshine of the spotless mind 

Twelve monkeys

Two of my favorite films!

6. Coherence 2013 & Triangle 2009 I love.
7. The Departed
8. Primer
9. Interstellar

## 2. Freeway (1997)

**Score:** 32
**Body:** Freeway (1997)

Reese Witherspoon is completely unhinged here and I loved every second of it! For someone who admires her and had only watched her in nice girl roles, I was happily shocked.

The story is a modern time urban retelling of Little Red Riding Hood; a runaway teen who crosses paths with a predator while going to her grandma's house. It has a number of plot twists, wild moments and comedy. Its really entertaining. And the ending is unexpected and interesting to say the least.

Kiefer Sutherland always rocks, but he gave me chills on this role.

And the movie gives food for thought, especially concerning the idea of a imperfect victim, child prostitution, the mirage of social respectability that protects predators and the failure of the system with impoverished young children and teens.

There is a sequel Freeway 2 (2000).



### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yeah it was very good. It's strange that it took me so long to hear about it. I had it in my head hat she made Fear and then had her big breakthrough with Pleasantville or Election lol. Most people I know associate her with those movies or Legally Blonde, Cruel Intentions etc ..it's just weird how it isnt brought up more often when she is discussed.
2. The sequel is absolutely batshit.
  - Hold the phone, there's a fucking sequel?
3. "I may not be able to move my neck, but I have not lost my smile"  Still hilarious
4. I love when she laughs her ass off at him in court!
5. I rewatched this movie a few weeks ago because I didn't trust my memory of it. Yep, it's as unhinged as I remembered.
6. I learned how to make toothbrush knives from Reese Witherspoon.
7. Reese's best performance
  - This and Election, but yes.
8. And Bokeem Woodbine as her boyfriend, Chopper! I love this movie and all its  white trash glory.

## 3. Movies for daddy/daughter date night?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Girls are aged 5 and 7, and I'm a millennial. What are some nostalgic, kid appropriate movies that little girls will enjoy?

They currently like: the sandlot, honey I blew up the baby, the Disney live actions, descendants, etc.

I tried harry potter and they enjoyed it, but I'm afraid it gets too scary by the 3rd movie.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Have they seen Mulan?
2. Troop Beverly Hills (1989)
3. Princess Bride
4. A Little Princess (1995)
5. *A League of Their Own* (1992)
6. Honey I Shrunk the Kids, Homeward Bound, The Neverending Story, ET, Toy Story
7. Enchanted!
8. Hook.  One of the best daddy films out there.
9. My Neighbor Totoro

Williy Wonka

The Barbie animated movie
10. Honey I Shrunk the Kids

The Secret Garden

Annie

The Parent Trap

Matilda

## 4. Thursday (1998): One of the most underrated crime thrillers of the 90s

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Do you want a hidden gem?
Check out Thursday (1998). If you like 90s crime thrillers in the vein of Pulp Fiction or True Romance, this is a must-watch.
It’s a stylish, fast-paced story about an ex-drug dealer whose peaceful suburban life is upended when his old partner shows up. It’s gritty, darkly comic, and packed with tension. Thomas Jane and Aaron Eckhart are brilliant.
Seriously underrated and definitely worth your time.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I still have the VHS.  My punk band actually took a clip from this movie as an intro/interlude on our album in 2001
2. I used to think Thomas Jane and Aaron Eckhart were the same person.
3. The fact that this just slipped out by kino while the dvd was so fucking rare is insane.
  - True!
4. Paulina Porizkova is not hard to look at either.
  - That's right, and she plays her role fantastically 😁👌🏼.
5. Great Movie! Just saw it once by accident playing on tv many years ago. Damn and that Polina was hot
6. Haven't seen this, but people who like it should probably also enjoy City of Industry (1997).


7. [removed]
8. What kinda vibes you after? If it's thrillers, Prisoners is underrated af.

## 5. Any movies where something feels off the whole time, then the ending confirms it?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I’m looking for movies where there’s this constant subtle wrongness in the atmosphere, like you can’t fully trust what you’re seeing, or something in the narrative feels slightly misaligned and then the ending either reveals the truth or recontextualizes everything. 

For example, The Sixth Sense (1999), Jai Bhim (2021), Shutter Island （2010), Memento（2000）, Stillwater (2021)

Prefer crime/ investigation movies but any genre are welcome.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fight Club (1999)
2. Rosemarys Baby
3. Gone Baby Gone
4. The Others

Sorry to bother you - this one takes a turn towards the end, but there's more movie after the disclosure...
5. The Invitation (2015)
6. Jacob’s Ladder
7. Bugonia
8. Get Out
9. Don't Look Now with Donald Sutherland. Nails this vibe. It's a classic suspense / light horror film from the 70s.
10. mulholland drive, burning

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Name an actor who totally surprised you and actually made a movie better?

**Score:** 2081
**Body:** Tom Arnold made True Lies better and totally surprised me as I thought he was only Rosannes ex.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/jmvixcmpf6mg1.jpeg?width=313&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=ff88c4f2f37ef941dcb9a6771dfbed1b58f8896e
  - Shut the fuck up, Dale!
    - ![gif](giphy|3ETNFinSwBdwQ)
2. Loved Tom Arnold in True Lies. Because of this movie I was always excited to see him in other films, and somehow he never channeled that charisma again. Kind of a one hit wonder. Perhaps other filmmakers didn’t know how to properly utilize him, I don’t know.
  - Excuse me, he killed it in the restroom scene in Austin Powers. 
    - https://preview.redd.it/xv832ykee6mg1.jpeg?width=360&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=b954272c69a31b9d0f92419b417cea3203c636fa
3. James Marsden in Sex Drive.
4. What kind of sick bitch takes the ice cube trays out of the freezer?
5. Tom Cruise in Tropic Thunder
6. Not a movie but holy shit did Colin Farrell kill it as Oz in the show The Penguin. Don't get me wrong I always thought he was a decent actor but wow his performance was unbelievable. He transformed himself 

## 2. This scene was hilarious, and the movie overall is really fun. What do you think about Superbad?

**Score:** 751

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's one of the best movies about male teen friendships. It's honest.

On top of being hilarious.
2. “It’s YOU, McMuffin!!” 😆 that line killed me
3. They let you pick any name you want when you get down there. 

And you landed on McLovin? 

It was either that or Mohammed.
4. Gotta catch a glimpse of these warlocks
  - She had back problems man
5. Movie is hilarious, the bit with the dick drawings kills me every time.
6. https://preview.redd.it/393qze5c09mg1.jpeg?width=2316&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=bc65bb70acc650275b2dece81c818c1de92fd0f6

I met McLovin recently.

This movie was on repeat in my house during my college years.
7. Where's all the stolen liquor, Danny Ocean?  Did you hide it under your butt?
8. You scratch our backs, we'll scratch yours.

Well Jules, the funny thing about my back is that it's located on my cock.
9. ![gif](giphy|aWGVYnrPV8YRa)

## 3. Is it worth watching???

**Score:** 273

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes, immediately.
  - I gotta finally watch this an check it off my watchlist!
2. If you are curious about the true events, this movie does a great job of explaining the situation.
3. Great movie, great acting, awesome screenplay!
4. For me, it's in my top 5
5. One of the best movies of the century
6. Crime not to watch
7. No way you asked that question. Watch it right away. Top notch performances and direction.
8. This movie is so good. Little to no side plot; no BS love interest; no fat whatsoever. Just the crimes and the Globe blowing the roof off about it. It’s insane how much was covered up and how many of these men of god were willing to look the other way
9. Absolutely.

Possibly one of the best screenplays of the 21st century.

## 4. Worst Accents in Your Favorite Films?

**Score:** 254
**Body:** The American accents in ‘In Bruges’ are pricelessly bad. 😂

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Quentin Tarantino doing an Australian accent in Django Unchained.
  - Shut up bleck!
  - He was harnessing his inner South African
2. Whatever Jon voight was doing in that anaconda movie.
  - "Joooo wanna play ruff, Anaconda?" 


3. I can't believe no one has mentioned Dick van Dyke's Cockney accent in Mary Poppins. 


What i find hilarious was an interview with him where he was asked about it. He had no idea how to do a Cockney accent so he took a stab at it. He was the only non British actor on the set and was shocked years later to learn that the accent was so horrible and was baffled as to why no one ever told him he was doing it wrong. 
4. "Nyet! Pey dat myan hees myonney!"
5. Nothing is worse than Richard Gere's abysmal attempt at a brogue in ***The Jackal***, though Tom Cruise in ***Far And Away*** and Brad Pitt in ***The Devil's Own*** are both up there. Brad Pitt later redeemed himself with his brilliant performance in ***Snatch*** though.
6. Cameron Diaz in Gangs of New York. Just... awful.
7. Keanu in Dracula

## 5. Slightly obscure movies you loved growing up

**Score:** 136

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Toy Soldiers, man I watched this so many times growing up. Nice pull, Op
  - POTS AND PANS!
  - That movie had such a great cast. Wil Wheaton and Sean Astin were perfect for those roles.
2. Yes Center Stage!

![gif](giphy|WrHmDQzQgNb5zV9h5O|downsized)
3. Without a Paddle was somehow one of my favorite movies as a kid lol, I’m sure owning the dvd helped
4. Time Bandits!!!
5. You randomly just made me think of The Incredible Mr. Limpet.   

How?
6. I loved Center Stage and watched a ton in college. A young Zoe Saldana was funny and sassy.
7. Clockstoppers

Timeline

Iron Will

The Last Unicorn

Bedknobs and Broomsticks

Ferngully

Oliver and Company
8. Toy Soldiers is one of my nostalgic favourites. Absolutely love it!

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The 'controversy' over 'Three Billboards Outside of Ebbington, Missouri' is because of people taking things too literally

**Score:** 62
**Body:** Spoilers, obvs, for a film that came out 9 years ago. 



I'll be honest, literalism is my biggest gripe over the last 15 years about films across the board. But this film I actually stayed away from because I heard it basically redeemed a really racist, horrible cop character, just to make the audience (esp white audience) feel good about themselves. That made it sound trite and boring to me. 

But here I was with a sick day saturday to myself, and it was on a streamer, so i watched it. 

It was one of the most devastating portraits of grief and anger that I've ever seen. It reminded me of Blue Ruin and Revanche to name a couple others. Mildred is sort of your hero at the start--who wouldn't feel bad and want to root for a grieving mother?--but as the film goes on you see how her grief has turned her into someone that hurts others a lot, including her surviving son. I wasn't 'rooting' for her in the way I root for like, the main character in Get Out to escape the racist cannibals. I felt for her, and hoped she would find peace. 

Then there's Sam Rockwell's Jason Dixon. Just a complete scumbag, who isn't even good at his job. It's not like he's a great detective, he is just also racist and violent. No, he's kind of a terrible cop, too. 

This is where, though, I was waiting for this cheesy redemption arc. And it never came. 

Yes, he tries to solve the murder, but... he fails? and he does it in a boneheaded, stupid way? 

Then he and Mildred set out to murder that soldier who also busted up Mildred's store earlier. And yeah, maybe he raped and burned a girl while serving overseas but... we don't know that, they don't know that. Even he did, though: wasn't the whole thing we went through in 2020 about restorative justice, abolition of prisoners, and defunding the police, about how cops shouldn't be the judge jury and executioner? 

And if you don't think Martin McDonagh, the filmmaker KNEW this, then you haven't read or seen his other works. McDonagh is the anti-Tarantino. He makes violent movies and plays to show how freaking futile it is to spend your life on revenge. Just like the teenage bride says (from her bookmark about 'polio or polo' lol) anger begets greater anger. It doesn't mean Mildred is 'bad', no, a great film shouldn't be caught up in labeling someone good or bad, so the audience can pat themselves on the back for being 'one of the good ones'. It certainly doesn't make Dixon 'good', cuz he definitely isn't! I'd argue that going to Idaho to murder a guy is just more of the same for him! 

A redemptive arc for Dixon would be, like, going to therapy, and then becoming a therapist himself or something. Not committing vigilante justice. 

So this film was incredible for me, and it wasn't always fun to watch, because of how tragic it was. But I'm glad I finally saw it. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I didn’t love this movie (it was okay) but I always thought this argument against it was so weak. We were not supposed to walk away from it thinking Sam Rockwells character was a hero or redeemed or anything like that. I question the critical thinking skills of anyone who came away from the movie thinking that was what it was trying to make them think. 
  - Yea, the whole point is learning to live together in the shadow of problems you are incapable of fixing. Rockwell’s character ends the story still an awful, violent, crooked cop. The world is full of people like him, and you can’t wish them away. But sometimes, you can come to an understanding with them.

It’s a bit of a shame for me, because it’s got a fantastic premise and themes, an A+ cast, a lot of powerful moments, and so many interesting ideas.  I think it just got let down by the details.
2. Luckily I wrote something about this in 2018, so I can accurately remember what I thought about a movie I haven't seen since then. :)

Most of the complaints seem to center around the character of Dixon and whether he is "redeemed" at the end. I think this criticism not only misses the point of the ending but reveals that the critics of this point might be unaware of what really offends them about it.

The movie isn't about Dixon and his arc, it's about Mildred and her arc. She begins as a righteously angry grieving mother fighting 'the man' with a bold public move, and ends in a morally dubious plan, partnering with a violent racist creep. In this way, the movie can be read as a scathing critique of the Justice Warrior and a warning about how easy it is for self-righteously blinding rage to morph into something unsavory, counter-productive and quite the opposite of how it was intended. I feel like what many of the critics of the politics of the movie are reacting to is how it reflects on themselves, "the good guys," rather than how it muddies its portrait of "the bad guys."  
  
At the end, with rage leading the line, the difference between "good guys" and "bad guys" is neglible.
3. Once a film becomes a symbol of an issue in "the culture", discourse about it gets divorced from the work itself.


Not to say that movies can't validly be criticized on non-artistic grounds (ex: political or whatever). It's just that once you move from an aesthetic discourse into another one, the actual qualities of the film get ignored in favor of the point or signal you're trying to make.
4. The moral panic around the film and Rockwell’s character was so overblown and overwrought that it truly felt like satire at the time for anyone with even a half-working brain lmao

I cannot fathom how you could watch that film, let alone understand Mcdonagh as a writer/filmmaker, and come away with the idea that he is glorying or supporting the actions of an abusive police system.

It was just low-information viewers hopping on the nebulous concept of “police bad” to pay themselves on the back about their sociopolitical anxieties at the time.

Three Billboards was and is a fantastic film in the catalogue of a fantastic filmmaker.
5. I guess I'll swim against the tide here for a minute: I've watched and read McDonagh's plays, I think his movies are more hit or miss, but generally I am a fan of his work. I think his work is best is situated in the environment he's most familiar with, where his black humor and a kind of misanthropy works well there--or I'm not Irish enough to pierce how it doesn't work. When he started writing more American characters, specifically in his play Behanding at Spokane, he had the racist character say the N-word. The play very much revolved around the old racist guy and plied a lot of the humor on how wrong he was, but also sensanalist excitement at the character getting to say all the nasty things. And admittely common combination in McDonagh's work. Toby, the black man, the lone character, was narratively structurally just something to riff off--to add more tension to those moments when Carmichael (the racist old dude) said things. A prop more or less, a critique which the play landed even while generally successful.

By the time Three Billboards comes out, it gets the cricistism it gets. I'm not going to rehash everyone's memories of the best or worst arguments--this is why it didn't work for me from what I remember. I think it used racism as color to Rockwell's character--a feature flattened out by McDonagh's general mode of a dim view of humanity. It's not a full-throated redemption because McDonagh makes racism just another thing, without unique characteristics to one of his old Irish bastards. But it isn't, it has it's own contours, it has different rules and experiences about how allowed and not allowed it was. Charitably, I think McDonagh knows he can't write a black American character that well, so he didn't. Compare it so say, Sophia Coppola, who with the Beguiling decided she couldn't handle the black characters in the novel's plantatation setting because she didn't think she could handle them, the tropes and stereotypes, by having them leave. A Virginia plantation during the Civil War. I don't think McDonagh's is as empty a gesture as that, but I think the minimizing done--perhaps earnestly because of lack of confidence regarding the story he could tell, amounts to erasure. Tools to prop up a story, but in the gesture showing a weakness either of nerve or of the tellling/
6. My interpretation: that soldier was absolutely the murderer. The whole movie has themes about people overlooking the flaws of their own people, and taking steps to protect their people regardless of right/wrong or legality. So the army officer guy claiming his solider was overseas when the cops call asking is just one more example of this theme. He would know what his soldier was capable of, so naturally he wouldn’t hand his guy over. 

We already know the soldier had a sure connection to Mildred and the daughter’s story, otherwise why’d he come to the store and wreck stuff?

I have zero doubt at all that the soldier did it, and I don’t think it was even ambiguous. The only question in the end is whether Mildred and Dixon will go through with killing the soldier. And I think they do.

The movies not about redemption, it’s about human tribalism, and Dixon has found his new tribe. Just my own interpretation. Personally I loved it.
7. It's a great movie with great actors.  I'm confused.  I don't think Rockwell's character was the issue with the redemption arc.  I'm pretty sure it was Woody Harrelson's character.   Isn't it something like SPOILERS  he only concedes because he knows he's dying and wants to spend time with his kids?  Basically he didn't have a change of heart,  more like a "who GAF? I'm dying anyway"  but otherwise would have been right on board with the racist cop?

I could be wrong.  I'm off to google.  
  - I watched this for the first time only a few hours ago and I can see why people might have issues or questions about Harrelson's character.

(Further spoilers)
On the surface he does seem to repent his backwards mentality and to try and be the savior, but everything he does is out of a misguided understanding of others.  He encourages Rockwell's aspirations and ignores the glaring character flaws, he kills himself to spare his wife and kids from associating him with bad memories but in turn he only sours their last good memory of him.  

I felt his "redemption" was only for his own gain.  His revenge is a "funny haha" by paying for another month of the billboards, leaving a broken home, and giving false hope.  Even the note he tapes to the hood was selfish, he knew the wife would find him.
    - Yes.  This is what I remember.   It's one of those weird things when an actor is beloved by the audience, in general.   They seem to want to always see them as somehow a good guy "deep down inside" no matter how terrible his actual actions and the consequence of the actions are.  I remember being weirded out that people were acting like he was the hero to the main character, when he basically devastated his own family.   The billboards and the entire story were petty in comparison.

The most egregious example of this IMO was Neegan from Walking Dead. SPOILERS   He literally bashed Glenn's head in, in front of his pregnant wife with a barbed wire wrapped baseball bat.   But the actor, Jeffrey Dean Morgan, is charming and loved by the audience.  So they tried a "redemption" arc later and then even a spin off where he and Maggie have to go to NYC.  And we're supposed to watch it like "Wow, look what happens when she's got the badass on HER side this time!  Sweet!"

It's like....um... NO.  NO, you don't get to "come back" from the baseball bat.  There's no redemption arc in the entire universe that makes that work.   Yet the audience loved him.  Really creeped me out.  But again, it's only a tv show.  It's only a movie.  The show isn't really the issue. It's what it says about the audience IMO.

## 2. How can I develop better critical thinking skills and form my own opinions about films?

**Score:** 48
**Body:** Hey guys, I've recently become more interested in movies and games and I've realized I'm way too easily influenced by what I read online. If I watch a movie and love it, but then read a review pointing out technical errors or plot holes I didn't see, my experience "dies"i fell kind of guilty about it,both for not having noticed that detail before and also for having lost my opnion again. It's like I discard what I felt at the time and start agreeing with the critic, as if my perception was wrong for not noticing those details before. It happened today: I finished a game mod and thought it was amazing. I excitedly went to see what people thought on Discord and it was all just trashing the mod. Instantly, I lost interest in the game and even gave up on commenting on it. I wanted to know if you also feel that the "community's" opinion sometimes dictates what you should like, and how you manage to separate critical thinking from personal enjoyment without letting the internet(or me)ruin the fun.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Write your own review before you read anyone else's. If you can't write a review, write bullet points why you like it. You are allowed to have an opinion and stick with it. Are you the same with food, do you stop eating stuff you like because other people don't like it.
2. Write. Grab a Lettrboxed account and just write at least a little bit about every film you watch. 

At the same time, don't feel bad for not seeing things other people see in films. Everyone's different and comes to a film with a different experience and perspective. Yours will get more varied and dynamic over time. 
  - To expand on this, write about what you found amazing, express what makes you feel good before reading what others found bad. It helps arm yourself against those thoughts by simply having a counter to them that you've yourself expressed. 

I've taken to this process. It helps formulate your arguments and your opinion into something concrete. 

Also don't why away from having a contrary opinion. Expressing yourself can also change someone else's mind.
3. For your own opinion, you just need to be confident in your own reaction to a film. Great movies have “plot holes” and generally reviews focusing on plot holes in the abstract are probably not good reviews. A plot hole is bad if it detracts from what the film is going for - if there’s a plot hole in a Hitchcock movie, it’s probably because that part of the plot wasn’t that important to the overall story or feel.

To develop better critical abilities, to analyze movies, it takes reading in depth reviews that go through their analysis that way… looking at the film and how the camera/actors/score/sets etc all aid that story or vibe of the movie (or not.) It can also be helpful to know some film theory - or at least a passing familiarity of it - so you can breakdown something you’ve watched or look at it through a particular (or multiple) critical framework. There are a lot of introductory film books and I’m sure you can find YouTube videos about breaking down a film for analysis.
4. My number one tip is reading, read other peoples analysis and eventually your brain will start to form patterns. Read critical theory/frameworks to help broaden your horizons. Watch films more than once so you can develop deeper thoughts on what you're seeing and pay attention to different aspects like theming, subtext, visual motifs and so on.

My number two tip (and apologies if this is a self plug) try watching my Youtube channel. It's in depth analysis that not only explains how things are being achieved and why, but will also teach you things like history, classical literature, things the films are referencing and so on. Here if you want it: [https://www.youtube.com/@Novum\_YT](https://www.youtube.com/@Novum_YT)

EDIT: Sorry I forgot, most crucially don't hold your opinions up to others, 'what the mainstream thinks' etc. If you love something and everyone hates it, try to figure out what spoke to you that didn't speak to them. There are many phenomenal pieces of art that the mainstream has no time for.
5. I think those are two different scenarios really

A film critic (in a perfect world) will have insights from decades of industry experience, so it’s not a lack of critical thinking thats causing you to change your opinion, but rather a lack of experience. If anything, being able to change your opinion shows GOOD critical thinking skills as you are considering the merits of outside viewpoints to alter your existing view

The more you know, the harder it is to enjoy simple things. Like, once you go to film school, you watch films differently. Same with listening to music if you’re a composer or eating food after going to culinary school

Your second situation is a bit different imo because it’s just a bunch of people on Discord trashing a game you like. Maybe their arguments have valid points? But these things usually come from a place of burnout or tribalism in my experience

I suppose at the end of the day it comes down to how much merit you place on other people’s opinions. I do think this is a spectrum though and takes practice to end up in a healthy place where you remain open to outside opinions without completely abandoning your own takes
6. Hey yeah I completely agree, but think maybe an exercise in critical thinking is to really respect on the review and whether it really resonates with your experience. 

Interestingly I think some films are 'growers', we watch them, think about them for a few days and develop an opinion. Perhaps reading a lot in between isn't always a good thing.

Recent example - I watched 'bird' with little expectation, I thought it was beautifully shot but very weird and not my usual thing. Then I read lots of rave reviews and something in me did an about turn and I started recommending it to people.
7. Time and experience. Learning how movies work. Listening to what people love from their favorite movies, learning from real life itself. There are, of course, truly bad movies, but there are also a lot of movies that are unfairly praised or condemned because they did or did not resonate with the critic in question. Sometimes, talking to people and understanding why they like x movie makes you more capable to appreciate the movie yourself and form your own opinion.

TL;DR: Take time to know yourself. Listen to people talking about what they love and why.
8. Maybe you need to let the impression settle a bit. If you're still in the process of digesting the movie, so to speak, and you mix other movie-adjacent input\* in form of others' opinions or analysis of the movie, it alters or even disturbs that process.

Now, some movie resonate more, or more deeply than others. Movies that speak to you, which appear to have depth to you, will probably take longer to process than others.

If anything, I'd recommend not seeking out input like that unless you kind of...have a craving or an appetite for it.

Like, when I watch a movie that doesn't really hit me in any particular way, and I'm analytical about it anyway, instead of being immersed and experiencing it, I'm often interested in others' opinions right afterwards. Seeing what I missed, or if there's someone who really described what I was thinking in a neat way that makes me think.

But with movies that resonate a little more deeply, I usually wait until I'm sure how I feel about it. Which to me is a matter of confidence and overall movie-watching experience. As in, how many movies you've taken in over the course of your life and how well you're able to interpret your feelings towards one. Which can also sometimes happen during a movie. Even if I'm totally hooked, I catch myself already "quantifying" (if you will) how I feel about it at that moment, knowing fully well that doesn't have to mean much, since the movie isn't over yet. Sometimes an ending, or a whole last act can really save or destroy a movie that was good or kinda bland before.

But as soon as I feel confident in how I feel about it, I look for stuf others said. Either because I know what I think can't be swayed, or because I feel like there's something missing, and others' opinions can help me fill that gap. Like, I'm sure about whether or not the part I'm not sure about yet needs external input. And yes, I could probably watch a couple of times over until more of my own analysis comes in, but it can often jog my memory, reading others impressions. Making me remember things at the same time as seeing it from a different, more distant, rational angle. Which always feels kind of like cutting corners to me, but if I want to form my opinion now, or write something down (something coherent), I'm fine with that.  




\*With that I mean adjacent to whatever particular movie you're in the proces of didesting, of course.
9. Just stick with your first impression. If a movie works for you, it works. There is absolutely nothing else you need to do. Looking at a movie through someone else’s perspective can be interesting but is not necessary. It just sounds to me like you need to be more confident in your own experience

## 3. We Are All Strangers: The Joys and Sorrows of an Ordinary Singaporean Family, the Ups and Downs of Life, the Hardships and Marginalization of the Vulnerable, a Cinematic Representation of Social Issues in Singapore, and the Shared Emotions and Conditions of Humanity

**Score:** 10
**Body:** On February 19, 2026, I watched the Singaporean film We Are All Strangers(《我们不是陌生人》), which was screened at the 76th Berlin International Film Festival(Berlinale). This film, which tells the joys and sorrows of an ordinary Singaporean family, is sincere in emotion and rich in detail, and it moved me deeply. Therefore, I write this brief review to share my reflections.

The film takes as its main thread the stories of two couples. The middle-aged couple Boon Kiat and Bee Hwa, played by Andi Lim and Yeo Yann Yann, and the young lovers Junyang and Lydia, played by Koh Jia Ler and Regene Lim, both enter into marriage amid twists and turns. Yet before and after marriage, they are troubled by livelihood pressures, and their relationships evolve from simplicity to complexity, unfolding a dramatic tragicomedy of life’s ups and downs.

Family affection and romantic love are the most prominent themes of the film. Boon Kiat and Junyang are a father and son who depend on each other for survival. Like many teenagers, Junyang is rebellious, yet his father is always willing to tolerate and embrace him. When Junyang and his girlfriend “get into trouble” with an unexpected pregnancy and the girl’s family comes to their door, the financially strained Boon Kiat would rather borrow from loan sharks than allow his son’s wedding to be anything less than respectable.

Boon Kiat and Bee Hwa, this middle-aged couple, move from mutual affection to becoming husband and wife, experiencing the awkwardness of youth, the restraint of adulthood, and the mutual understanding and tolerance of an old married pair. From their marriage to Boon Kiat’s death, less than two years pass, yet their bond is deeply devoted, vividly illustrating the sentiment that even a short-lived marriage can carry affection as deep as the sea.

Junyang and Lydia’s romance and marriage, however, move from “dry tinder meeting flame” to gradual dullness, from throwing themselves into love without hesitation to passion fading away while livelihood worries become unavoidable. From carefree youth untouched by sorrow to words held back, even to facing each other in silence, with only tears streaming down. Yet as passion recedes and troubles multiply, the relationship, tested by hardship, becomes deeper and more layered. This is also the transformation many people experience from adolescence to adulthood, from young lovers to husband and wife.

An even more pivotal relationship is the familial bond between Junyang and Bee Hwa. The rebellious Junyang dislikes and looks down upon Bee Hwa, this “stepmother” who came from the background of a hostess, and he often offends her with his words. But after Boon Kiat falls ill and passes away, Bee Hwa manages the household, sells goods with forced smiles, and later takes responsibility for selling fake medicine on Junyang’s behalf and goes to prison. Only then does Junyang painfully realize that he has lost such a good mother. Bee Hwa is usually sharp-tongued and free-spirited, but in major matters she shows real courage and responsibility. Although Junyang is not her biological son, she loves him as her own—not merely out of a sense of elder responsibility, but as a mother’s love for her child, willing to take the blame and be imprisoned for him.

Such stories of family affection and romantic love are indeed not especially novel, yet I was still deeply moved. In particular, Yeo Yann Yann’s superb acting brings Bee Hwa, a mature and resilient woman, vividly to life. The personal experiences and family backgrounds of the characters also resonated strongly with me, as someone with similar experiences and circumstances, and I found myself in tears at the unfolding of the story.

The film also vividly presents many distinctive features and details of Singapore: 

Although prosperous and affluent, there are still many who struggle to make a living, selling not only their labor but also their dignity;

The HDB flats (组屋,public housing) that provide shelter for ordinary people; 

The hawker centres(食阁) that offer affordable food and are filled with everyday bustle; 

The dual nature of neighborly and workplace relationships in public housing estates and hawker centres, where gossip and competition coexist with mutual help and warmth; 

The widespread Christian faith and religious wedding ceremonies; 

The “A-Level”examinations that place enormous pressure on many Singaporean students and parents; 

Those on the margins of society struggling to survive, who may fall into vicious cycles with a single misstep; 

Discrimination and distance from the upper class toward ordinary people; 

Wealthy Chinese visitors who come to Singapore for enjoyment, spending lavishly while lacking integrity; 

The frightening violence of local Ah Long(大耳窿) loan sharks in debt collection.

In the film, Junyang’s family goes through many ups and downs, separations and deaths, wavering repeatedly between hope and despair. Though the plot is somewhat dramatized, overall and in its details it reflects the real lives and hardships of ordinary Singaporeans, including material deprivation, spiritual confusion, and the struggles and dilemmas that arise from them.

There is a scene in which Junyang’s family sits together watching the celebration of Singapore’s 60th anniversary of nationhood on television, with President Tharman greeting the crowds amid flowers and prosperity. Boon Kiat and Bee Hwa sigh at how wealthy Singaporeans appear, yet despite their hard labor, they still cannot afford a home truly their own. Later, when Junyang sees seafront apartments primarily sold to mainland Chinese tycoons, he is astonished—an emotion clearly shaped by the contrast with his own cramped living conditions.

Recently, the term “cut-off line”(斩杀线) has circulated in the media. The experiences of Junyang’s family in the film happen to reflect that, in a certain sense, such a “cut-off line” also exists in Singapore. Of course, the film employs dramatization, deliberately emphasizing tragic elements and blending various negative events. Yet in daily Singaporean news, one often reads reports of the poor falling into high-interest debt, being harassed by gangs, becoming involved in scams and other crimes, ending up in prison, and seeing their families fall apart.

In the film, Junyang’s family, like many people in real life, make one wrong step that leads to wrong steps after wrong steps, mistakes made in haste, a downward slide in life, and the more one struggles, the deeper one sinks into the mire. The saying that misfortune befalls those already suffering is not mere coincidence; in despair, people’s material poverty and psychological pain can damage and disrupt body and mind, making them prone to irrational actions and producing certain inevitable consequences.

Although Singapore has relatively sound housing, healthcare, and educational guarantees, there is still room for improvement in areas such as basic income, elderly support, and childrearing, and the wealth gap is also worrying. Singapore values meritocracy; the visibility and voice of lower- and middle-class citizens are insufficient. The government and social atmosphere encourage personal striving and competitive success, but striving does not necessarily bring success, and competition inevitably produces losers. The protections afforded to vulnerable ordinary people are relatively limited. 

Today’s social welfare system can ensure that citizens have food and a place to live, but if Singaporeans want to live more freely, with greater dignity and ease, they need not only extraordinary effort but also family background and luck, rather than something most people can achieve simply by working step by step.

In the film, the family of four are all living with hardship, experiencing life’s turbulence and the warmth and coldness of human relations. Junyang ultimately inherits his father’s occupation, which also means that, after being tempered by hardship, he accepts ordinariness: he changes from someone willing to take risks and seek shortcuts for a better life into someone who sets aside ideals for daily necessities, doing more laborious and humble but steady work. This is also the fate of most ordinary people. Class mobility is not easy, and effort does not necessarily lead to success. Random risks and accidents can easily destroy a person’s prospects. In the tides of history, ordinary people can only drift with the current; faced with harsh realities, they have to lower their heads, accept fate, and compromise.

The ending of the film is neither a complete happy ending nor a tragedy, but rather the ordinary ups and downs inevitable in common lives, the fluctuations within life’s struggles. Junyang and Lydia’s child is also raised in a public housing flat and may grow up to share the same class and similar destiny as the parents—or perhaps not. Everything is possible, which also means it is uncertain and full of variables.

We Are All Strangers allows the world to see the stories of ordinary Singaporeans. The film not only draws international attention but may also help many Singaporeans recognize the “elephant in the room”—the social issues happening around them yet overlooked, the compatriots ignored due to poverty and marginalization, the forgotten corners of human life—and reflect upon them.

When people see the story in the film and understand the predicament of the weak, the suffering of the marginalized, and the helplessness of those struggling to live, they may move from misunderstanding to understanding, from exclusion to tolerance, from indifference to care. Although one cannot expect cinema alone to remedy deep-rooted human flaws and structural social problems, a film can nevertheless prompt reflection and emotional response, preparing the ground for certain positive changes in reality.

Whether public officials or members of civil society, all may thereby gain a fuller understanding of the many facets of society, foster empathy for others, strengthen solidarity among citizens, and even deepen the connection between human hearts and lived realities across all humanity—better addressing the problems that cause suffering and making necessary changes to structural deficiencies. In this way, everyone may live with greater security and dignity, striving for self-improvement while sustaining one another through mutual care and assistance. This is precisely the meaning and aspiration embodied in the film’s Chinese title We Are Not Strangers(我们不是陌生人), which stands in contrast to its English title We Are All Strangers.

Of course, I have also heard some criticisms of the film. For example, that the plot is somewhat conventional, certain developments are predictable, and while it touches on many issues, most are only explored superficially. These problems do exist, and I felt similarly while watching. Yet its flaws do not obscure its merits. The film’s strengths far outweigh its weaknesses. In particular, its emotional scenes are sincere and moving, and its depiction of reality deeply touches the heart, sufficient to cover its shortcomings.

As a Chinese viewer, watching a predominantly Chinese-language film allows me to empathize more deeply than with non-Chinese films, to reflect more, and to be more profoundly moved. I believe many other native Chinese-speaking viewers would feel similarly. 

Moreover, the livelihood stories and realities depicted in Singapore are also occurring in China; many of Singapore’s social issues are similar to, or even more severe in China. The images and voices in this Singaporean film objectively also speak on behalf of many Chinese people. For this reason, I have paid particular attention to and offered particular praise for this film.

The author of this review is Wang Qingmin(王庆民), a Chinese writer based in Europe. The original text was written in Chinese.

## 5. War of the Worlds (2005)- Did Rachel know what her father did?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** A question I always had about this movie is whether she fully realizes what Ray did in the scene where he implicitly kills the man who’s risking their safety (after covering her eyes and ears and making her sing over it). She does seem to hear some muffled sounds of the fight and instinctively holds her ears tighter, but she doesn’t have much context for what’s happening (she wouldn’t have heard Ray threaten the guy earlier as he was keeping his voice down so she wouldn’t hear) and she seems pretty calm and innocent afterwards- she doesn’t even look toward the other room, she doesn’t ask her dad what happened or show any fear or discomfort toward him, and he’s not generally a violent person so I’m not sure her mind would jump straight to the conclusion that he murdered him.

Do you think she knows the full truth, does she think it was handled nonlethally (e.g. that her dad seriously hurt him or left him in a coma, but didn’t kill him) or does she sense the guy’s dead but think it was self-defense as opposed to deliberate murder? You can see the scene here for context: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IMqarXIdYSM&t=77](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IMqarXIdYSM&t=77s)s
